steps.title: Find the median positions in data set A
steps.content:
For 50 players, the median is the average of the 25th and 26th values. Cumulative frequencies from the histogram:
• [30, 40): 4 players
• [30, 50): 11 players
• [30, 60): 39 players
Both positions 25 and 26 fall between 11 and 39, placing them both in the [50, 60) interval.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement I: Median B less than Median A?
steps.content:
When we add the value 18 (less than all existing data), it becomes the first value in sorted data set B.

This shifts every original value up one position. The median of the 51-value data set B is the 26th value, which corresponds to the original 25th value from A.
For Statement I to hold: $\text{(25th value)} < \frac{\text{(25th value)} + \text{(26th value)}}{2}$, which requires the 25th and 26th values to be different.

However, both values lie in the same interval [50, 60), so they could be identical.

In that scenario, the medians would be equal. Statement I is not necessarily true.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement II: Mean B less than Mean A?
steps.content:
Let $S$ be the sum of all values in A. Then:
Mean A $= \frac{S}{50}$ and Mean B $= \frac{S+18}{51}$
Testing Mean B < Mean A: $$\frac{S+18}{51} < \frac{S}{50}$$ Cross-multiply: $50(S+18) < 51S$ which gives $S > 900$.
From the histogram, the minimum possible sum is $4(30) + 7(40) + 28(50) + 11(60) = 2460$. Since $2460 \gg 900$, this inequality holds for all possible data sets. Statement II must be true.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content: Only Statement II must be true for all possible data sets matching the histogram. That matches choice B — B is correct.
